text_blocks.text: Mountains of the Long Range in
Newfoundland reach heights of nearly 3,000 ft (900
m). In the Chic-Choc and Notre Dame mountain ranges
in Quebec, the higher summits rise to about 4,000 ft
(1,200 m) elevation. Isolated peaks and small ranges in
Nova Scotia and New Brunswick vary from 1,000 to
2,700 ft (300 to 800 m). In Maine several peaks
exceed 4,000 ft (1,200 m), including Mount
Katahdin at 5,267 feet (1,605 m). In New
Hampshire, many summits rise above 5,000 ft (1,500
m), including Mount Washington in the White
Mountains at 6,288 ft (1,917 m), Adams at 5,771 ft
(1,759 m), Jefferson at 5,712 ft (1,741 m), Monroe
at 5,380 ft (1,640 m), Madison at 5,367 ft
(1,636 m), Lafayette at 5,249 feet (1,600 m), and
Lincoln at 5,089 ft (1,551 m). In the Green
Mountains the highest point, Mt. Mansfield, is 4,393
ft (1,339 m) in elevation; others include Killington
Peak at 4,226 ft (1,288 m). Camel's Hump at 4.083
ft (1,244 m), Mt. Abraham at 4,006 ft (1.221 m),
and a number of other heights exceeding 3,000 ft
